title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the canal system. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: Water Highways: The Rise and Fall of the Canals
content.text: Before the arrival of the railway, the interior of continents remained largely isolated from the coastal ports. Moving heavy goods like coal, iron, and stone over land was a slow, expensive process involving muddy roads and teams of struggling horses. The solution was the canal—a man-made 'liquid highway' that could move massive loads with minimal effort. ||(41)|| [41]The 'Golden Age' of canals began in England in the 1760s with the Duke of Bridgewater’s canal[/41]. It was designed to carry coal from his mines directly into the heart of Manchester. The impact was immediate: the price of coal in the city dropped by half overnight, fueling the fires of the early Industrial Revolution. ||(42)|| [42]This success sparked a period of 'Canal Mania.' Investors poured money into hundreds of new projects, carving through hills and bridging valleys with spectacular stone aqueducts[/42]. The engineers who built them, like James Brindley and Thomas Telford, became the celebrities of their day, solving impossible topographical puzzles with sheer determination. ||(43)|| [43]To overcome the problem of changing elevations, they developed the 'lock' system[/43]. By creating a series of chambers with gates, they could 'lift' or 'lower' a boat like a staircase. This allowed canals to cross entire mountain ranges, connecting the Atlantic with the Mediterranean or the Great Lakes with the Hudson River. ||(44)|| [44]Life on the canals was a world unto itself[/44]. The 'narrowboats' were often the homes of the families who operated them, decorated with traditional folk art and brass fittings. It was a slow, rhythmic way of life, where the speed was dictated by the steady walk of the towpath horse. ||(45)|| [45]However, the dominance of the water highway was short-lived[/45]. By the 1840s, the steam locomotive had arrived. Trains were faster, could operate in freezing weather that locked the canals in ice, and were not limited by the availability of a water supply. One by one, the great canal companies began to go bankrupt. ||(46)|| [46]Today, many of these historic waterways have been reclaimed for leisure[/46]. The towpaths that once echoed with the sound of heavy hooves are now filled with cyclists and walkers, and the narrowboats have been converted into floating holiday homes. The canals are no longer the arteries of industry, but they remain a vital part of our landscape, a reminder of a time when the world moved at the pace of a walking horse.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, [41] a single horse [/41] could pull fifty tons of cargo in a boat, whereas it could only pull one ton in a cart on the road. This fifty-fold increase in efficiency made the massive investment in digging and engineering economically irresistible.
paragraphs.B: This [42] dramatic reduction in costs [/42] proved that water transport was the key to industrial growth. It wasn't long before other mine owners and factory bosses were demanding their own connections to the growing network.
paragraphs.C: However, [43] these ambitious routes [/43] required more than just digging. Engineers had to figure out how to move tons of water across uneven ground without it all draining away, leading to the development of sophisticated clay linings called 'puddle'.
paragraphs.D: These [44] mechanical stairs [/44] were a marvel of the age. They transformed the geography of trade, allowing the industrial heartlands of the English Midlands or the American Midwest to export their goods to the entire world.
paragraphs.E: Despite [45] the hard work and long hours [/45], a distinct culture emerged among the 'boaters.' They developed their own songs, slang, and traditions, often remaining a somewhat isolated community that was viewed with both curiosity and suspicion by those on land.
paragraphs.F: This [46] rapid decline [/46] saw many canals abandoned and filled in. Some were even paved over to become the routes for the very railways and motorways that had replaced them, effectively erasing the water from the map.
paragraphs.G: Many [47] modern environmentalists [/47] argue that we should return to canal transport for bulk goods. They point out that a modern barge is still more fuel-efficient than a fleet of trucks, offering a 'greener' alternative for the 21st century.

questions.explanation: A provides the mathematical proof for the 'minimal effort' mentioned in the introduction, comparing horse-drawn carts to boats.
B refers to the 'dramatic reduction in costs' (coal price dropping by half) described in the preceding paragraph.
C describes the engineering challenges of 'these ambitious routes' following the mention of 'Canal Mania' and celebrity engineers.
D uses the metaphor 'mechanical stairs' to refer to the 'lock' system described before the gap.
E expands on the life of the 'boaters' and their culture, following the description of the narrowboats as homes.
F explains the 'rapid decline' of the canals following the introduction of the steam locomotive.
